rationale: Every active project-scoped SoNash agent carries `skills: [sonash-context]` in YAML frontmatter. This is a load-bearing behavioral field that injects a named skill's content into the agent's context at spawn time. D13's 63-field catalog has no field for this. Absence means losing the spawn-time context-injection wiring for every project-scoped agent. JASON-OS agents drop this field on port because no equivalent skill exists yet — tracking it makes the gap visible and actionable.

D1c surfaced ported / partially-ported / sonash-only / not-portable as four distinct states. D1d confirmed the partial-port state (skill-audit v3.1 vs v4.0). D1e confirmed version-ahead case (session-begin JASON-OS v2.1 > SoNash v2.0). D13's `portability` field (portable/sanitize-then-portable/not-portable) answers HOW to move a unit; port_status answers WHERE IT CURRENTLY IS in the migration. These are orthogonal. port_status drives the work queue; portability drives the method. Enum: ported | partial-port | sonash-only | not-ported-portable | jason-os-only | in-sync.

Multiple agents surfaced non-trivial version gaps: skill-audit (JASON-OS v3.1, SoNash v4.0 — batch/multi modes absent), deep-plan (JASON-OS v3.0, SoNash v3.3 — convergence-loop integration absent), brainstorm (JASON-OS v1.0, SoNash v1.1 — phase gates absent), session-begin direction inverted (JASON-OS v2.1 AHEAD of SoNash v2.0). D13's `lineage` object captures provenance but not the functional gap. A delta string like 'SoNash v4.0 > JASON-OS v3.1 — batch/multi modes missing' is the actionable representation of version gap for sync tooling.

D1d's pr-review port documents 3 explicitly dropped reference docs (LEARNING_CAPTURE.md, SONARCLOUD_ENRICHMENT.md, TDMS_INTEGRATION.md) and 2 dropped reviewers (CodeRabbit, Gemini). D1e's session-end port strips entire Phase 3 (reviews:sync, TDMS, ecosystem health). D1e's session-begin port defers 8+ SoNash health scripts. This information is currently only in notes — making it a first-class array enables automated port-fidelity checks and surfaces functional gaps that the receiving project may want to fill. Each item: string naming what was dropped and why.

Five audit skills (audit-ai-optimization, audit-enhancements, audit-security, audit-performance, audit-engineering-productivity, audit-comprehensive) carry `supports_parallel: true/false` in YAML frontmatter. system-test has `supports_parallel: false`. This is not in SCHEMA_SPEC Section 3A or D13's catalog. It is a load-bearing frontmatter field affecting how the orchestrator decides to invoke the skill (parallel agent dispatch vs sequential). Sync tooling needs this to preserve orchestration behavior on port.

Paired with supports_parallel. audit-comprehensive, audit-ai-optimization, audit-security, audit-performance carry `fallback_available: true` — meaning a sequential fallback mode exists when the Task tool is unavailable or parallel mode is disabled. This is a distinct capability from supports_parallel: a skill can be parallel-capable but lack a sequential fallback (no degraded mode). Sync tooling should preserve this field to correctly characterize skill degradation modes.

Audit skill frontmatter carries `estimated_time_parallel: N min`. Observed values: 65 min (audit-comprehensive), 30 min (audit-ai-optimization), N min (audit-security, audit-performance). Together with estimated_time_sequential this forms a cost/time governance pair embedded in skill frontmatter — it helps orchestrators decide when parallel spawn cost is justified. Not in SCHEMA_SPEC or D13.

Paired with estimated_time_parallel. Observed values: 180 min (audit-comprehensive), 90 min (audit-ai-optimization), N min (audit-security). The gap between parallel and sequential time encodes the value of parallelism — audit-comprehensive is 3x faster in parallel mode. Not in SCHEMA_SPEC or D13.

system-test frontmatter carries `estimated_sessions: 6`. This is the only skill in the scan with a session-count estimate. For large audit or analysis skills that require multiple work sessions, this metadata helps operators plan project timelines. Not in SCHEMA_SPEC or D13.

system-test frontmatter carries `total_domains: 23`. For complex audit skills with numbered domain structures, this is a useful quick reference for scope. Audit skills without this field require reading the full SKILL.md to understand scope. Not in SCHEMA_SPEC or D13.
system-test frontmatter carries `total_checks: ~100` (string, not int, because it's approximate). For audit/test orchestration skills, this gives a quick scope signal. Not in SCHEMA_SPEC or D13.

D1b found market-research-reports references 5 dependency skills (research-lookup, scientific-schematics, generate-image, peer-review, citation-management) that do NOT exist in the SoNash repository. D13's `dependencies` array captures dependency names but cannot distinguish present vs absent. A separate `missing_dependencies` array makes broken dependency detection automatable — sync tooling can check this field without re-reading the file body. Critical for portability assessment when upstream dependencies don't exist.

D2a and D2b both flag `skills: [sonash-context]` as a non-standard YAML frontmatter field on agents. Preserving the raw frontmatter value (e.g., '[sonash-context]') is useful for migration tooling that needs to map SoNash skill injections to JASON-OS equivalents. Distinct from the structured `context_skills[]` field — this is the verbatim source, useful for audit and migration scripts. Nullable for agents without this field.

Multiple agents (D1a, D1b, D1c, D1d, D1f) surface the _shared/ dependency pattern: skills read from `.claude/skills/_shared/` shared markdown modules that MUST be ported alongside them. Whether a skill has a _shared/ dependency is a binary sync risk signal — a skill with has_shared_dep:true cannot be ported without also porting the relevant _shared/ files. Currently embedded in dependencies array strings but not queryable as a boolean.

Companion to has_shared_dep. When a skill has _shared/ dependencies, this array lists the exact paths (e.g., ['_shared/ecosystem-audit/CRITICAL_RULES.md', '_shared/ecosystem-audit/COMPACTION_GUARD.md']). D1a noted that tdms/pr/session ecosystem audits have _shared/ read directives baked into SKILL.md and cannot function without those files present. Making these explicit enables automated co-dependency grouping during sync planning.

D1b, D1c, D1d, D1e all confirm that SoNash skills with mature protocol invoke `cd scripts/reviews && npx tsx write-invocation.ts` in their closure phase. This TypeScript invocation registry is SoNash-only and must be stripped on every JASON-OS port. JASON-OS-ported skills systematically omit it. A boolean flag makes this pattern queryable — any skill with invocation_tracking:true needs a strip or stub before porting. D1e: 'use presence/absence of write-invocation.ts to flag ported vs SoNash-native more reliably than version number alone'.

D2a and D2b surface `model: inherit` as a valid and meaningful value — distinct from a hardcoded model ID. Agents using inherit (debugger, general-purpose, mcp-expert, performance-engineer, pr-test-analyzer, silent-failure-hunter, technical-writer) inherit the spawner's model. This is a cost-control mechanism: orchestrators can choose which model spawns these agents. D13 has a `model` field (SCHEMA_SPEC Section 3B) but its enum has no documented values — `inherit` needs explicit recognition. boolean for quick filtering; the `model` field carries the actual value.

D2a found 3 deprecated redirect stubs (deployment-engineer→fullstack-developer, devops-troubleshooter→debugger, error-detective→debugger). D2b found 7 more. D1f found 2 deprecated skill stubs (website-synthesis→synthesize, repo-synthesis→synthesize). All 12 carry a redirect target but D13's schema only captures status:deprecated and superseded_by. `redirect_to` is more specific: it names the ACTIVE target the user/skill should now invoke. superseded_by tracks conceptual lineage; redirect_to tracks the operational navigation target. These can differ (a skill can be superseded by a redesign while redirecting to a different but closer alternative).

D2a's 3 deprecated agent stubs and D2b's 7 stubs all carry explicit expiry dates (e.g., '2026-06-01'). A first-class field enables automated dead-redirect cleanup — sync tooling can exclude any stub whose expiry date has passed. Currently embedded only in body prose.

D2b surfaces a structural distinction between project-scoped agents (.claude/agents/*.md, have `skills: [sonash-context]`) and user-scoped agents (.claude/agents/global/*.md, no context injection, portable). This maps to the `scope` field (project vs user) but the distinction is significant enough for agents to warrant its own enum: `project | user | global`. The global/ subdir is an architectural tier difference, not just a path difference — global agents have zero SoNash coupling by design.

D1e notes debt-runner is currently not-portable but upgrades to sanitize-then-portable once TDMS infrastructure lands. D1b notes market-research-reports is sanitize-then-portable but broken without 5 missing dependency skills. These are conditional portability states — the unit's portability is gated on an external prerequisite. A prose field capturing the condition (e.g., 'Requires TDMS pipeline port first. See scripts/debt/ inventory.') enables forward planning and distinguishes 'never portable' from 'portable pending X'.

D1a surfaces that scoring.js and state-manager.js inside skill script bundles contain file-header comments 'Forked from pr-ecosystem-audit'. This fork provenance is not in any frontmatter — it is only discoverable via code comments. For sync, knowing a lib file is a fork (not an independent copy) affects how drift between fork and canonical should be handled. An array of fork-source paths enables automated drift detection. Applies to script-type records nested inside skill directories.

D1b notes market-research-reports uses `structure.md` where other skills use `REFERENCE.md`. The SCHEMA_SPEC `has_reference_md` boolean cannot represent this: has_reference_md:false would be wrong (structure.md IS a reference doc), has_reference_md:true would also be wrong (no file named REFERENCE.md exists). A `companion_docs` array listing all companion doc filenames (REFERENCE.md, structure.md, prompts.md, examples.md, domains.md) covers all variants and avoids the boolean-vs-actual-name ambiguity surfaced by D1b.

D1c notes skill-creator's Version History states 'Anthropic skill, Apache 2.0' — the ultimate origin is external to SoNash. D13's `lineage.source_project` would say 'sonash' (SoNash is the holding repo), but the upstream origin is Anthropic. A dedicated `upstream_origin` field (e.g., 'anthropic' | 'third-party' | 'sonash-original' | 'jason-os-original') distinguishes skills that were externally adopted into SoNash from skills that originated in SoNash. Affects licensing, attribution, and whether upstream updates should be tracked.

D1b notes market-research-reports has a Python script (generate_market_visuals.py) while all other skills use Node.js. The SCHEMA_SPEC `module_system` field is cjs/esm/none — it does not cover Python, Bash, TypeScript. SCHEMA_SPEC Section 3F has a `language` enum for tools (go/javascript/bash/yaml/toml/json/markdown). Skills-embedded scripts need the same field but for Python as well. This is distinct from tool-language because it applies to script files nested inside skill directories. Enum: javascript | python | typescript | bash | other.

D1c notes mcp-builder and artifacts-builder both have binary tar.gz files bundled inside their script directories (shadcn-components.tar.gz). Sync tooling must handle non-text files differently. A boolean flag enables sync tooling to detect skills requiring binary-aware transfer. Currently only discoverable by reading component file lists — not queryable from schema alone.

D1c notes the CAS handler skills (repo-analysis, website-analysis, media-analysis, document-analysis) store `Document Version:` in an HTML comment block BELOW the YAML frontmatter, not as a YAML field. A parser using only YAML frontmatter will miss the version for these 4 skills. The enum captures where version metadata lives: yaml_frontmatter | html_comment | version_history_table | none. Required for sync tooling that needs accurate version extraction without full-body reads.

D1c and D1d confirm that analyze, synthesize, recall, repo-analysis, website-analysis, document-analysis, media-analysis form an all-or-nothing Content Analysis System (CAS) cluster. These cannot be usefully ported in isolation — the SQLite backend + scripts/cas/ scripts + analysis.json schema contract + shared/CONVENTIONS.md must land together. A boolean flag on each member enables the sync tool to enforce cluster-atomic port decisions. D1d: 'treat all three as a bundle'.

D2b notes both team files include explicit token cost estimates: audit-review-team (~3x solo), research-plan-team (~4x solo). This embedded governance field helps orchestrators decide when teams are worth spawning. Currently in prose body — making it a first-class field enables programmatic cost gating. String not float because it is expressed as a multiple with qualifier (e.g., '~3x solo', '~4x solo').

D2b surfaces explicit spawn thresholds for both teams: audit-review-team gate is '3+ artifacts OR /audit-comprehensive'; research-plan-team gate is 'BOTH deep-research + deep-plan on same topic, L/XL complexity, multi-session plan'. These preconditions for team invocation are currently only in body prose — making them a first-class field enables automated spawn-eligibility checking in orchestrators. String (prose) not structured object because conditions are compound and context-dependent.

D2b surfaces that 3 GSD global agents use `mcp__context7__*` (wildcard) in their tools list rather than enumerating specific MCP tools. The SCHEMA_SPEC `tools` field is an array of specific tool names — a wildcard entry is not parseable as a specific tool. A separate `mcp_tool_wildcards` array (e.g., ['mcp__context7__*']) captures these permission class specifications without polluting the specific tools list. Enables sync tooling to verify MCP availability by wildcard pattern.

D2b surfaces the `.claude/agents/global/` subdir as a structurally distinct tier — user-scoped agents available across all projects, with zero SoNash coupling (no `skills: [sonash-context]`). The `scope` field would say `user` but agents in global/ have a different architectural meaning: they are designed to be project-agnostic from inception. A boolean flag enables quick identification of the 11 GSD agents as the strongest port candidates in the agent roster.

D1f notes the `schemas/` directory has no SKILL.md and contains only TypeScript code (Zod schemas + CLI validator). It should be classified as `script-lib` or `tool-file`, not `skill`. A field capturing the correct type when the unit lives under .claude/skills/ but is not a slash command enables correct downstream classification. This is a schema integrity field, not a portability field. Nullable for units correctly classified.
